Name: information_bottleneck_based_compression
Title: Exploring the Information Bottleneck in Model Compression through Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: The information bottleneck principle can be utilized to derive new rate-distortion bounds for model compression that account for task relevance, leading to more efficient coding schemes that better preserve predictive performance.
Related Work: Existing literature has explored the intersection of rate-distortion theory and compression (e.g., Zhang et al. 2021, Sefidgaran et al. 2022) but often view distortion purely in terms of reconstruction error. This proposal distinguishes itself by integrating the information bottleneck framework to emphasize task-relevant distortion, which has not been fully explored in the context of model compression. The aim is to derive achievable rate-distortion bounds that consider both the model's representational capacity and the specific task it serves.
Abstract: This research proposal aims to investigate the application of the information bottleneck principle to model compression through the lens of rate-distortion theory. Traditional model compression techniques often focus on minimizing reconstruction error without adequately considering the task relevance of the compressed representation. By framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem with task-oriented distortion measures, we will derive new theoretical bounds that quantify the trade-offs between model size, predictive performance, and task-specific requirements. We will explore the implications of these bounds for developing more efficient encoding schemes that prioritize task-relevant information. The expected outcomes include novel bounds on the bits-per-weight necessary to maintain predictive sufficiency, along with empirical validations on standard datasets. This work has the potential to advance the understanding of model compression in a way that is tightly linked to the practical demands of machine learning tasks.
Experiments: Conduct a series of experiments using standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, MNIST) to evaluate the proposed compression schemes against baseline methods.
Implement coding schemes based on the derived rate-distortion bounds, varying the level of distortion to observe the effects on predictive performance.
Evaluate the methods using metrics like classification accuracy, bits-per-weight, and the mutual information between input data and model predictions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the difficulty in accurately estimating task-relevant distortion measures, which may vary across different tasks. Additionally, the theoretical bounds may not translate directly into practical implementations without careful calibration. There is also a risk of overfitting to specific datasets, limiting generalizability.
